main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Mixture of Modality-Aware Experts (MoMa): a modality-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture for early-fusion mixed-modal language models","Division of expert modules into modality-specific groups (text experts and image experts) with learned routing within each group","Adoption of expert-choice routing for load balancing and static computation graph","Use of Chameleon as the base architecture with MoE applied at each layer"],"problems":["Improving the efficiency of pre-training mixed-modal, early-fusion language models","Addressing computational challenges in scaling mixed-modal early-fusion foundation models","Enhancing the ability of models to process and integrate information from both text and image modalities"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["FLOPs savings (overall, text, image)","Pre-training loss","Throughput reduction"],"results":[{"metric":"Overall FLOPs savings (MoMa vs dense reduction (MoMa vs dense baseline)","value":"-17%"},{"metric":"Overall FLOPs savings (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)","value":"3×"},{"metric":"Text FLOPs savings (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)","value":"3×"},{"metric":"Image FLOPs savings (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)","value":"2.8×"},{"metric":"Throughput reduction (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)","value":"-9%"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a modality-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture (MoMa) for early-fusion mixed-modal language models","Division of experts into modality-specific groups with learned routing within each group for semantics-based adaptivity","Demonstration of substantial pre-training efficiency gains (FLOPs savings) over both dense and standard mixed-modal MoE baselines in early-fusion settings","Extension of modality-aware experts to the mixed-modal, early-fusion language modeling setting, beyond prior work focused on encoders or masked language models"]}

methods: Mixture of Modality-Aware Experts (MoMa): a modality-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture for early-fusion mixed-modal language models
Division of expert modules into modality-specific groups (text experts and image experts) with learned routing within each group
Adoption of expert-choice routing for load balancing and static computation graph
Use of Chameleon as the base architecture with MoE applied at each layer
problems: Improving the efficiency of pre-training mixed-modal, early-fusion language models
Addressing computational challenges in scaling mixed-modal early-fusion foundation models
Enhancing the ability of models to process and integrate information from both text and image modalities

results.metric: Overall FLOPs savings (MoMa vs dense baseline)
Text FLOPs savings (MoMa vs dense baseline)
Image FLOPs savings (MoMa vs dense baseline)
Overall FLOPs savings (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)
Text FLOPs savings (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)
Image FLOPs savings (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)
Throughput reduction (standard expert-choice MoE vs dense baseline)

novelty_claims: Introduction of a modality-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture (MoMa) for early-fusion mixed-modal language models
Division of experts into modality-specific groups with learned routing within each group for semantics-based adaptivity
Demonstration of substantial pre-training efficiency gains (FLOPs savings) over both dense and standard mixed-modal MoE baselines in early-fusion settings
Extension of modality-aware experts to the mixed-modal, early-fusion language modeling setting, beyond prior work focused on encoders or masked language models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["MoE-Tuning: a three-stage training strategy for LVLMs with Mixture of Experts (MoE)","MoE-LLaVA: a sparse LVLM architecture using MoE layers and learnable routers, activating only top-k experts per token"],"problems":["High computational cost of scaling Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) due to dense activation of all parameters","Performance degradation when applying sparsity (MoE) to multi-modal LVLMs","Efficient scaling of LVLMs without sacrificing performance"],"datasets":["ScienceQA","POPE (object hallucination benchmark)","MMBench","LLaVA^W","MM-Vet"],"metrics":["Accuracy (ScienceQA, MMBench, MM-Vet, LLaVA^W)","Object hallucination rate (POPE)"],"results":[{"metric":"ScienceQA accuracy improvement over LLaVA-1.5-7B","value":"1.9%"},{"metric":"POPE (object hallucination) improvement over LLaVA-1.5-7B","value":"0.4%"},{"metric":"MMBench accuracy improvement over LLaVA-1.5-7B","value":"0.9%"},{"metric":"LLaVA^W accuracy improvement over LLaVA-1.5-7B","value":"30.7%"},{"metric":"MM-Vet accuracy improvement over LLaVA-1.5-7B","value":"3.8%"},{"metric":"POPE (object hallucination) improvement over LLaVA-1.5-13B (with 2.2B activated parameters)","value":"1.1%"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of MoE-Tuning, a novel three-stage training strategy to adapt MoE to LVLMs and prevent performance degradation from sparsity.","Proposal of MoE-LLaVA, a sparse LVLM framework that expands parameter count while keeping computational cost constant by activating only a subset of experts per token.","Demonstration that MoE-LLaVA achieves comparable or superior performance to much larger dense models (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-13B) with significantly fewer activated parameters, especially in object hallucination mitigation."]}

methods: MoE-Tuning: a three-stage training strategy for LVLMs with Mixture of Experts (MoE)
MoE-LLaVA: a sparse LVLM architecture using MoE layers and learnable routers, activating only top-k experts per token
problems: High computational cost of scaling Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) due to dense activation of all parameters
Performance degradation when applying sparsity (MoE) to multi-modal LVLMs
Efficient scaling of LVLMs without sacrificing performance

results.metric: POPE (object hallucination) improvement over LLaVA-1.5-13B (with 2.2B activated parameters)

novelty_claims: Introduction of MoE-Tuning, a novel three-stage training strategy to adapt MoE to LVLMs and prevent performance degradation from sparsity.
Proposal of MoE-LLaVA, a sparse LVLM framework that expands parameter count while keeping computational cost constant by activating only a subset of experts per token.

Demonstration that MoE-LLaVA achieves comparable or superior performance to much larger dense models (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-13B) with significantly fewer activated parameters, especially in object hallucination mitigation.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Comprehensive ablation studies on image encoder, vision-language connector, and pre-training data choices","Careful mix of image-caption, interleaved image-text, and text-only data for pre-training","Scaling up model size (3B, 7B, 30B parameters) and exploring mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures","Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) after pre-training","Design and training of MM1 family of multimodal models"],"problems":["How to build performant Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)","Identifying the importance of architecture components and data choices in MLLMs","Understanding the impact of different types of pre-training data on few-shot, zero-shot, and text-only performance","Lack of transparency and design principles in existing MLLM development"],"datasets":["COCO 2014 validation set","12 established multimodal benchmarks (not all named explicitly)"],"metrics":["Few-shot accuracy","Zero-shot accuracy","Captioning performance","Visual Question Answering (VQA) performance","Pre-training metrics"],"results":[{"metric":"Few-shot performance (captioning and VQA)","value":"MM1 outperforms Emu2, Flamingo, and IDEFICS in both small and large model size regimes"},{"metric":"Pre-training metrics","value":"MM1 achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results"},{"metric":"Performance after SFT on 12 benchmarks","value":"Competitive with SOTA models"}],"novelty_claims":["Comprehensive and transparent ablation of architecture and data choices for MLLMs, which is rare in the field","Identification of the relative importance of image encoder, image resolution, and pre-training data types for MLLM performance","Demonstration that vision-language connector design is of negligible importance compared to other factors","Scaling up to a family of models (MM1) with both dense and MoE variants up to 30B parameters","MM1 achieves SOTA few-shot results across multiple benchmarks and demonstrates strong in-context and multi-image reasoning abilities"]}

methods: Comprehensive ablation studies on image encoder, vision-language connector, and pre-training data choices
Careful mix of image-caption, interleaved image-text, and text-only data for pre-training
Scaling up model size (3B, 7B, 30B parameters) and exploring mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) after pre-training
Design and training of MM1 family of multimodal models
problems: How to build performant Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)
Identifying the importance of architecture components and data choices in MLLMs
Understanding the impact of different types of pre-training data on few-shot, zero-shot, and text-only performance
Lack of transparency and design principles in existing MLLM development
datasets: 12 established multimodal benchmarks (not all named explicitly)

results.value: MM1 outperforms Emu2, Flamingo, and IDEFICS in both small and large model size regimes

novelty_claims: Comprehensive and transparent ablation of architecture and data choices for MLLMs, which is rare in the field
Identification of the relative importance of image encoder, image resolution, and pre-training data types for MLLM performance
Demonstration that vision-language connector design is of negligible importance compared to other factors
Scaling up to a family of models (MM1) with both dense and MoE variants up to 30B parameters
MM1 achieves SOTA few-shot results across multiple benchmarks and demonstrates strong in-context and multi-image reasoning abilities

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Unified multimodal Transformer network with shared parameters for vision and language","Integration of modality-aware sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) modules for modality-specific information processing","Masked signal modeling as a unified pre-training task for both image and text (reconstructing masked pixels and tokens)"],"problems":["Scalable and efficient vision-language pre-training","Learning from diverse, multimodal data with a unified model","Reducing training time and resource requirements for large vision-language models"],"datasets":["Not explicitly listed in the provided content, but mentions evaluation on visual question answering, visual reasoning, and image-text retrieval tasks (likely standard datasets such as VQA, NLVR2, MSCOCO, Flickr30k, etc.)"],"metrics":["Training speed (acceleration factor)","Downstream task performance (e.g., accuracy, retrieval metrics)"],"results":[{"metric":"Training speed","value":"4x faster than models pre-trained with Image-Text Contrastive and Image-Text Matching losses"}],"novelty_claims":["Unified multimodal Transformer pre-trained with a single masked signal modeling objective for both vision and language","Integration of modality-aware sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) modules within the shared Transformer network","Significantly accelerated training (4x faster) compared to previous contrastive/matching loss-based approaches","State-of-the-art performance on multiple vision-language downstream tasks with a simpler and more scalable architecture"]}

methods: Unified multimodal Transformer network with shared parameters for vision and language
Integration of modality-aware sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) modules for modality-specific information processing
Masked signal modeling as a unified pre-training task for both image and text (reconstructing masked pixels and tokens)
problems: Scalable and efficient vision-language pre-training
Learning from diverse, multimodal data with a unified model
Reducing training time and resource requirements for large vision-language models

metrics: Downstream task performance (e.g., accuracy, retrieval metrics)

results.value: 4x faster than models pre-trained with Image-Text Contrastive and Image-Text Matching losses

novelty_claims: Unified multimodal Transformer pre-trained with a single masked signal modeling objective for both vision and language
Integration of modality-aware sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) modules within the shared Transformer network
Significantly accelerated training (4x faster) compared to previous contrastive/matching loss-based approaches
State-of-the-art performance on multiple vision-language downstream tasks with a simpler and more scalable architecture

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Vision Mixture of Experts (V-MoE): a sparse version of the Vision Transformer (ViT) where some dense feedforward layers are replaced with sparsely activated mixture of MLP experts","Batch Prioritized Routing: a new routing algorithm that prioritizes and skips computation for less informative image patches, enabling adaptive per-image compute and smooth trade-off between performance and compute at inference time"],"problems":["Scaling vision models efficiently to very large parameter counts without incurring prohibitive computational costs","Reducing inference and training compute for large vision models while maintaining or improving accuracy","Enabling conditional computation in vision models, where only a subset of parameters are used per input"],"datasets":["ImageNet"],"metrics":["Test accuracy","Training FLOPs (floating point operations)","Inference compute/runtime","Few-shot and full fine-tuning accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Test accuracy on ImageNet (15B parameter V-MoE)","value":"90.35%"},{"metric":"Training FLOPs saved compared to dense models","value":"20%"},{"metric":"Inference compute reduction (to match dense model performance)","value":"as little as half of the compute"},{"metric":"Model size","value":"up to 15B parameters"}],"novelty_claims":["First demonstration of scaling sparse Mixture of Experts models to vision tasks at the scale of 15B parameters, the largest vision models to date","Introduction of V-MoE, a sparse Vision Transformer variant that matches or exceeds dense model performance with significantly less compute","Proposal of Batch Prioritized Routing, a novel routing algorithm for adaptive per-image compute in vision models","Demonstration that V-MoE can be smoothly adjusted at inference time to trade off between performance and compute","Stable training and transfer of very large sparse vision models, including successful fine-tuning with as few as 1,000 datapoints"]}

methods: Vision Mixture of Experts (V-MoE): a sparse version of the Vision Transformer (ViT) where some dense feedforward layers are replaced with sparsely activated mixture of MLP experts

Batch Prioritized Routing: a new routing algorithm that prioritizes and skips computation for less informative image patches, enabling adaptive per-image compute and smooth trade-off between performance and compute at inference time
problems: Scaling vision models efficiently to very large parameter counts without incurring prohibitive computational costs
Reducing inference and training compute for large vision models while maintaining or improving accuracy
Enabling conditional computation in vision models, where only a subset of parameters are used per input

results.metric: Test accuracy on ImageNet (15B parameter V-MoE)
Training FLOPs saved compared to dense models
Inference compute reduction (to match dense model performance)
results.value: as little as half of the compute

novelty_claims: First demonstration of scaling sparse Mixture of Experts models to vision tasks at the scale of 15B parameters, the largest vision models to date
Introduction of V-MoE, a sparse Vision Transformer variant that matches or exceeds dense model performance with significantly less compute
Proposal of Batch Prioritized Routing, a novel routing algorithm for adaptive per-image compute in vision models
Demonstration that V-MoE can be smoothly adjusted at inference time to trade off between performance and compute
Stable training and transfer of very large sparse vision models, including successful fine-tuning with as few as 1,000 datapoints

inferred from context, but not explicitly named in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (for classification tasks such as VQA, NLVR2)","Retrieval metrics (e.g., Recall@K, though not explicitly named in the provided text)"],"results":[{"metric":"State-of-the-art accuracy on VQA","value":"Achieved (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"State-of-the-art accuracy on NLVR2","value":"Achieved (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"State-of-the-art performance on image-text retrieval","value":"Achieved (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Faster inference speed on retrieval tasks compared to fusion-encoder models","value":"Yes"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a unified model (VLMo) that can function as both a dual encoder and a fusion encoder for vision-language tasks.","Introduces the Mixture-of-Modality-Experts (MoME) Transformer, which uses modality-specific experts and shared self-attention within each block to flexibly encode images, text, and image-text pairs.","Presents a stagewise pre-training strategy that leverages large-scale image-only and text-only data in addition to image-text pairs, improving generalization and representation learning.","Demonstrates that the unified model achieves state-of-the-art results on both retrieval and classification tasks, outperforming previous dual-encoder and fusion-encoder models."]}

methods: Unified Vision-Language Pretrained Model (VLMo)
Mixture-of-Modality-Experts (MoME) Transformer
Stagewise pre-training strategy (image-only, text-only, image-text pairs)
Joint pre-training with image-text contrastive learning, image-text matching, and masked language modeling
problems: Unified modeling for both vision-language retrieval and classification tasks
Efficient and effective cross-modal representation learning
Leveraging large-scale image-only and text-only data for vision-language pre-training
Overcoming limitations of dual-encoder and fusion-encoder architectures
datasets: Image-text retrieval datasets (e.g., MSCOCO, Flickr30K; inferred from context, but not explicitly named in the provided text)
metrics: Accuracy (for classification tasks such as VQA, NLVR2)
Retrieval metrics (e.g., Recall@K, though not explicitly named in the provided text)

results.metric: Faster inference speed on retrieval tasks compared to fusion-encoder models

novelty_claims: Proposes a unified model (VLMo) that can function as both a dual encoder and a fusion encoder for vision-language tasks.
Introduces the Mixture-of-Modality-Experts (MoME) Transformer, which uses modality-specific experts and shared self-attention within each block to flexibly encode images, text, and image-text pairs.
Presents a stagewise pre-training strategy that leverages large-scale image-only and text-only data in addition to image-text pairs, improving generalization and representation learning.
Demonstrates that the unified model achieves state-of-the-art results on both retrieval and classification tasks, outperforming previous dual-encoder and fusion-encoder models.

methods: VL-MoE: a large-scale generative Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) multimodal model for vision-language tasks
Scaling both text-based and vision-based feed-forward networks (T-FFN and V-FFN) using MoE in a unified framework
Dividing the model into multiple sub-models (experts) specialized for modal-specific subsets of input data
Aligning text and vision input representations via three mask data modeling objectives
Exploring scaling strategies: increasing dense model size, increasing number of experts, and scaling T-FFN or V-FFN alone
Ablation studies to analyze model behavior and interpretability
problems: Scaling vision-language models efficiently while maintaining or improving performance
Reducing computational cost of large-scale vision-language models
Stabilizing training of large-scale MoE models for multimodal tasks
Improving interpretability and understanding trade-offs in scaling VLMs

results.value: Competitive with dense models using similar or more pre-training data and 3-4x more parameters per token

novelty_claims: First large-scale generative Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) multimodal model for both vision/language-only and vision-and-language tasks
Unified scaling of both text and vision feed-forward networks with MoE in a single architecture
Comprehensive exploration of scaling strategies (dense size, expert number, T-FFN/V-FFN scaling) for VLMs
Ablation studies on model behavior, interpretability, and design choices in multimodal MoE models

methods: Early-fusion token-based mixed-modal modeling (Chameleon)
Uniform transformer-based architecture for both images and text
Quantization of images into discrete tokens for unified token representation
Novel architectural modifications (e.g., query-key normalization, revised layer norm placement) for stable mixed-modal training
End-to-end training from scratch on interleaved mixed-modal data
Adaptation of supervised finetuning for mixed-modal alignment
problems: Unified modeling of multimodal (image, text, code) documents with arbitrary interleaving
Overcoming limitations of modality-specific encoders/decoders in multimodal models
Stable and scalable training of early-fusion mixed-modal models
Achieving state-of-the-art performance across both vision-language and text-only tasks
Enabling high-quality mixed-modal reasoning and generation in a single model
datasets: Text-only benchmarks (e.g., commonsense reasoning, reading comprehension datasets)
Long-form mixed-modal generation evaluation (new human evaluation dataset)
metrics: Human preference rate (for long-form mixed-modal generation)

results.metric: Human preference rate vs Gemini-Pro (long-form mixed-modal generation)
Human preference rate vs GPT-4V (long-form mixed-modal generation)

results.value: Outperforms Flamingo, IDEFICS, Llava-1.5 (exact value not specified)

novelty_claims: First early-fusion, token-based mixed-modal foundation model capable of arbitrary interleaving and generation of images and text in a single sequence.
Unified transformer architecture for all modalities, trained from scratch without modality-specific encoders or decoders.
Novel architectural and training techniques (e.g., query-key normalization, revised layer norm placement) enabling stable and scalable mixed-modal training.
First large-scale human evaluation of open-ended mixed-modal reasoning and generation, demonstrating superior performance to much larger models (e.g., Gemini-Pro, GPT-4V).
Chameleon sets a new bar for open multimodal foundation models, enabling full multimodal document modeling and generation.

methods: Training and releasing OpenMoE, a series of open-sourced decoder-only Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models (LLMs) with varying sizes (650M to 34B parameters)
In-depth analysis of MoE routing mechanisms, including context-independent specialization, early routing learning, and drop-towards-the-end phenomena
Training with a substantial proportion of code data (up to 52.25% in early pre-training)
Exploring the UL2 training objective as an alternative to next-token prediction
Releasing intermediate checkpoints for research purposes
Proposing potential strategies to mitigate identified routing issues in MoE models
problems: Lack of open-sourced, reproducible MoE-based LLMs trained on trillion-level diverse datasets
Understanding the cost-effectiveness trade-off between MoE-based and dense LLMs
Limited understanding of MoE routing mechanisms and their impact on model performance, especially in post-ChatGPT LLMs
Performance degradation in sequential tasks due to imperfect routing (e.g., token drop-towards-the-end)
Challenges in training LLMs efficiently without increasing computational resources
datasets: Pre-training data (over 1 trillion tokens, including a substantial proportion of code)
metrics: Comparative performance with OpenLLaMA-3B and TinyLLaMA-1.1B (dense LLMs)

results.value: OpenMoE-8B/32E achieved comparable performance to OpenLLaMA-3B and TinyLLaMA-1.1B at lower training cost

novelty_claims: First open-sourced and reproducible decoder-only MoE LLMs trained on up to over 1 trillion tokens, with models ranging from 650M to 34B parameters
In-depth analysis of MoE routing mechanisms, revealing context-independent specialization, early routing learning, and drop-towards-the-end phenomena
Release of intermediate checkpoints to facilitate further research
Exploration of advanced training strategies, including high code proportion in pre-training and the UL2 training objective
Identification of key issues in MoE routing and proposal of potential solutions for future MoE LLM development

Achieves state-of-the-art text-to-image generation with significantly less training compute than previous methods.

Shows unprecedented controllability in multi-modal tasks after SFT.

Demonstrates significant performance improvements and achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple document layout analysis datasets

methods: Expert Choice Routing in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models (experts select top-k tokens instead of tokens selecting top-k experts)
Heterogeneous MoE with variable number of experts per token and fixed bucket size per expert
problems: Load imbalance in conventional MoE routing leading to under- or over-specialized experts
Inefficiency and sub-optimal training in sparsely-activated MoE models due to poor routing strategies
Lack of variable expert allocation per token in prior MoE methods
metrics: Downstream task performance (accuracy, F1, etc. on GLUE and SuperGLUE tasks)

results.metric: Training convergence time (8B/64E model) vs. Switch Transformer top-1 and GShard top-2 gating
Downstream task performance (GLUE and SuperGLUE, 8B/64E model vs. T5 11B dense)
results.value: Outperforms T5 11B dense in 7 out of 11 tasks

novelty_claims: Proposes expert choice routing where experts select top-k tokens, guaranteeing perfect load balancing without auxiliary loss.
Allows variable number of experts per token, unlike prior fixed top-k token routing.
Demonstrates substantial gains in training efficiency and downstream performance compared to prior MoE routing strategies.
Shows strong scaling with increased number of experts and improved performance on downstream tasks.

methods: Reformulating Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) computation as block-sparse operations
Training dropless-MoEs (dMoEs) that never drop tokens
Developing high-performance GPU kernels for block-sparse matrix products using blocked-CSR-COO encoding and transpose indices
Implementing the MegaBlocks system on top of MegatronLM for efficient MoE training
problems: Inefficiency of current MoE training frameworks on GPUs due to dynamic routing and load imbalance
